items.q: Why did war break out after the 1947 UN Partition Plan?
items.correct: Jewish leaders accepted partition but Arab states and Palestinians rejected it, and fighting followed Israel's 1948 declaration of independence.
items.misconception: Both sides accepted the UN plan peacefully in 1947.
items.random_wrong: Britain rejected the partition plan and invaded.
items.why: Students assume agreement; the plan was accepted by one side and rejected by the other, leading directly to war.

items.q: What was the outcome of the Six-Day War in 1967?
items.correct: Israel won decisively in six days, seizing large territories including the West Bank and Gaza, which remain central to the conflict.
items.misconception: The Arab states defeated Israel in 1967.
items.random_wrong: The Six-Day War lasted six years.
items.why: Learners misjudge the result; Israel's rapid victory and territorial gains, not Arab success, define the war's significance.

items.q: Did the Oslo Accords of 1993 end the conflict?
items.correct: They created limited Palestinian self-rule and mutual recognition but left major issues unresolved, so the conflict continued.
items.misconception: The Oslo Accords brought a final, lasting peace.
items.random_wrong: The Oslo Accords created an independent Palestinian state.
items.why: Students overstate Oslo; it was a framework that left borders, refugees and Jerusalem unsettled, and peace did not hold.

items.q: What did the UN Partition Plan of 1947 propose?
items.correct: Dividing Palestine into Jewish and Arab states
items.random_wrong: British rule for fifty more years
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dividing Palestine into Jewish and Arab states. “A single Arab state” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What do Palestinians call the events of 1948?
items.why: Correct answer: The Nakba (catastrophe). “The Six-Day War” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did Israel capture in the Six-Day War of 1967?
items.correct: The West Bank, Gaza, Sinai and Golan Heights
items.why: Correct answer: The West Bank, Gaza, Sinai and Golan Heights. “Egypt and Jordan entirely” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did the 1978 Camp David Accords achieve?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peace between Egypt and Israel. “A Palestinian state” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Six-Day War take place?
items.why: Exact answer: 1967 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
